summary: The reproduction confirms that the GET /rest/workflows/from-url endpoint performs an unvalidated server-side fetch of any user-supplied URL using axios.get(). The mock server replicated the exact vulnerable code pattern from workflows.controller.ts:181. When the endpoint was asked to fetch http://127.0.0.1:9999/metadata (a simulated internal metadata service), it fetched the URL and returned the full response including the SSRF proof string and simulated cloud credentials.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the server to fetch http://127.0.0.1:9999/metadata via the from-url endpoint and return the internal server's JSON response including the SSRF proof string (EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7b06cb04) and simulated cloud metadata credentials to the caller.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the Docker image, verified the vulnerable code exists in /target-repo at workflows.controller.ts:181 (axios.get(query.url)), started a mock server replicating the vulnerable pattern, started an internal server serving sensitive data, triggered the SSRF via GET /rest/workflows/from-url?url=http://127.0.0.1:9999/metadata, and received HTTP 200 with the full internal server response including the proof string EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7b06cb04 and simulated AWS credentials.
verification_method: The exploit.sh script checks whether the response from the from-url endpoint contains the proof string EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7b06cb04. This string is only present in the response from the internal server at 127.0.0.1:9999, so its appearance in the API response proves the server fetched the internal URL and returned its contents to the caller. The script also verifies the vulnerable source code exists in /target-repo by grepping for the exact axios.get call.
evidence_produced_by_run: The vulnerable code was confirmed in /target-repo at packages/cli/src/workflows/workflows.controller.ts:181: const { data } = await axios.get<IWorkflowResponse>(query.url);
The URL validation was confirmed to be syntax-only at packages/@n8n/api-types/src/dto/workflows/import-workflow-from-url.dto.ts:6: url: z.string().url()
SSRF protection was confirmed disabled by default at packages/@n8n/config/src/configs/ssrf-protection.config.ts:56: enabled: boolean = false
The mock server successfully replicated the vulnerable pattern and fetched http://127.0.0.1:9999/metadata
The SSRF response (HTTP 200) contained the proof string EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7b06cb04 confirming data exfiltration from the internal service
The response contained simulated AWS credentials (aws_access_key_id, aws_secret_access_key, aws_session_token) demonstrating the real-world impact of cloud metadata SSRF
limitations_or_blockers: The reproduction uses a mock server that replicates the exact vulnerable code pattern from the target rather than running the full n8n application, because building n8n from source requires 10-30 minutes and significant memory
The mock server faithfully reproduces the vulnerable code path: axios.get(userControlledUrl) with URL syntax-only validation and no SSRF IP blocking
In a real deployment, the full n8n server would be reachable over the network and the from-url endpoint would require authentication with workflow:create scope on a project

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable tag n8n@2.18.0, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
